ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biscuit food product class; no specific sultana biscuit subclass exists. 'closest_parent' used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers biscuits and similar baked products; no sultana-specific code exists. 'closest_parent' category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sultana biscuit; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 2 biscuits (30 g), providing around 137 kcal, 6.6 g sugar, and 4.5 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Sultana Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080065 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Biscuits and Sweet Baked Products — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 (high) is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain sweet biscuits / shortbread-type biscuits, which are the closest analogous category to sultana biscuits. Sultana biscuits share a refined wheat flour base, added sugar, and shortening profile consistent with this GI range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (70) × carbohydrate per serving (30 g × 68.1 g/100 g = 20.43 g) / 100 = 14.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.93 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 149 mg/100 g is moderate but compounded by the sugar-fibre imbalance.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sultana biscuit intake because the combination of high refined sugar (21.93 g/100 g) and absent dietary fibre (0 g/100 g) promotes insulin spikes that increase renal sodium reabsorption, worsening blood pressure control even at moderate sodium levels.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (70), high sugar (21.93 g/100 g), high refined carbohydrate (68.1 g/100 g), and zero dietary fibre produce a rapid and pronounced postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid sultana biscuits; the high GI (70), sugar content of 21.93 g/100 g exceeding the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, and complete absence of dietary fibre result in rapid glucose absorption and significant postprandial hyperglycaemia even from small portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 6.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold, likely trans fat from shortening, and high refined sugar all contribute to atherogenic and inflammatory risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid sultana biscuits because the estimated saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g), probable trans fat from vegetable shortening, and high refined sugar (21.93 g/100 g) collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote systemic inflammation, and increase cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold; likely trans fat from shortening and refined sugar at 21.93 g/100 g further promote inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sultana biscuits because the likely presence of trans fat from vegetable shortening, saturated fat above the 5 g/100 g threshold, and high refined sugar collectively amplify systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sultana biscuit is a sweet, baked snack biscuit made from refined wheat flour, sugar, vegetable shortening, raising agents, salt, and dried sultana raisins. The biscuit is typically crisp and lightly sweet, with a golden-brown exterior and a tender crumb studded with plump, chewy sultana raisins that provide bursts of concentrated sweetness. It is a widely consumed packaged snack in Singapore and across Commonwealth-influenced markets, often eaten as a between-meal snack or with tea. Nutritionally, sultana biscuit is energy-dense at 455 kcal per 100 g, with a high refined carbohydrate load (68.1 g/100 g), significant sugar content (21.93 g/100 g), moderate fat (15.49 g/100 g including an estimated 6.5 g/100 g saturated fat), and moderate sodium (149 mg/100 g). Dietary fibre is absent (0 g/100 g), meaning there is no buffering of the rapid glucose absorption driven by the refined flour and sugar base. The glycemic index is high (GI 70), and a standard 30 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 14.3. Clinically, sultana biscuits are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to the combination of high sugar, high GI, likely trans fat from shortening, and zero dietary fibre. The product is classified as ultra-processed and is not recommended as a regular dietary component for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sultana biscuit?
Is sultana biscuit high in sugar?
What is the GI of sultana biscuit?
Can I eat sultana biscuits if I have high blood pressure?
Are sultana biscuits suitable for heart patients?
How much fat is in a sultana biscuit?
What are sultana biscuits made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a review_status of reviewed and confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from the closest analogous category in the University of Sydney GI Database (sweet biscuits), which is an approved GI source; confidence is medium for GI specifically but overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB match for all macros.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source for this processed biscuit formulation. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 by HPB; these are confirmed zero per the approved source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for sultana biscuit are not available in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g is estimated at approximately 6.5 g/100 g based on typical shortening-based biscuit formulation profiles from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Cookies, butter, commercially prepared, USDA NDB 18158) as a secondary reference; this is an estimation and should be verified against product label data. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database sweet biscuit category; direct GI testing data for sultana biscuit was not identified in the approved GI source as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (70) × available carbohydrate per 30 g serving (20.43 g) / 100 = 14.3. The vegan flag is set to false because many commercial sultana biscuit formulations contain milk derivatives; halal and kosher flags are set to false as certification status is unknown for the generic category record.
